extra.message: SQL injection in search route: user-controlled input from request.args.get('search', '') is directly interpolated via f-string into an SQL text() fragment. Attackers can inject arbitrary SQL by crafting a malicious search parameter, e.g., ' OR 1=1 -- to enumerate all records.

SQL injection in validate_token(): user-controlled registration code (from form.registration_code.data at line 45) is directly interpolated via f-string into a SQL query passed to session.execute(). An attacker can craft a malicious registration code value to inject SQL, e.g., ' OR '1'='1 to bypass token validation.

Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in the 404 error handler. Flask's render_template_string() is called with a string that includes request.path via f-string interpolation. An attacker can visit /{{config}} or other Jinja2 expressions to evaluate arbitrary template code. This can lead to RCE via Jinja2 sandbox escape techniques (e.g., {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}).

Insecure deserialization via Python pickle. The 'preferences' cookie (lines 114-118) is base64-decoded and deserialized with pickle.loads() without any integrity checking. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve arbitrary code execution on the server. The toggle_darkmode route (line 105) serializes preferences back to the cookie using pickle.dumps(), confirming the cookie is pickle-based.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in download() function. Called from routes/account.py:60-61 with user-supplied URL (form.url.data). urlopen() is called on the user-supplied URL without any validation, allowing an attacker to make requests to internal network resources (e.g., http://localhost:8080/admin, http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ on cloud providers).

Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via the |safe Jinja2 filter on note.text. Note text is user-controlled content created through CKEditor (which produces HTML). The |safe filter disables Jinja2 auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes when any user views the home page.

Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via safe_columns=['text'] in Bootstrap-Flask's render_table. The 'text' column is rendered without HTML escaping, allowing stored XSS through note text. The same pattern exists in templates/personal_notes.html:16.

Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in delete_note(). Any authenticated user can delete any note by its ID. There is no check that note.user_id == current_user.id, allowing users to delete other users' notes including admin private notes.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in get_personal_notes(). Any authenticated user can view any user's personal notes by simply changing the user_id in the URL path (e.g., /accounts/2/notes). There is no authorization check to ensure the requesting user owns the requested notes, and no filter on the private field, so private notes of other users are exposed.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: Mass assignment / privilege escalation vulnerability. The AccountForm includes an 'is_admin' BooleanField, and the filtered_values dict only excludes the 'password' key (line 79). When current_user.__dict__.update(filtered_values) is called, a regular authenticated user can set is_admin=True to escalate privileges to administrator. The template in templates/account.html:26-34 also confirms this field is sent from the client side.

cwe: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

extra.message: Missing @login_required decorator on account update POST route. The GET handler for /account has @login_required (line 19) but the POST handler at line 68 does not. While current_user would be anonymous for unauthenticated requests, this bypasses access control and could lead to unexpected behavior or exploitation.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Missing @login_required decorator on darkmode toggle POST route. This route sets a signed/unsafe cookie via pickle and can be triggered by any unauthenticated user.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Hardcoded, weak Flask secret key ('super secret key'). This allows session forgery — an attacker can forge Flask session cookies to impersonate any user, including admin. Combined with the insecure pickle deserialization (routes/account.py:118), this significantly broadens the attack surface.

Hardcoded static registration code ('a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764') that allows anyone to register an account. Combined with hardcoded default user passwords 'user' and 'admin' (lines 17-19), this provides predictable credentials for initial access.

Information disclosure via /is_logged_in endpoint. This endpoint is accessible without authentication and reveals whether a session is authenticated along with the current user's email address. This allows attackers to enumerate valid user accounts.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: SQLAlchemy echo=True logs all SQL statements (including query parameters) to stderr. In production, this could expose sensitive data such as passwords, registration codes, and note content in server logs.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

extra.message: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via safe_columns=['text'] in Bootstrap-Flask's render_table. Note text is rendered without HTML escaping, allowing stored XSS through note text.

The 404 error handler includes request.path in the rendered error message via render_template_string(). This leaks the attacker's exact request path in the response body, aiding reconnaissance. However, the primary threat from this code is SSTI (reported separately).

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: Potential XSS via profile_image data attribute in <object> tag. current_user.profile_image.decode() is rendered without escaping. If an attacker controls their profile image data by uploading a non-image URL via the SSRF-prone add_image route, the raw response bytes are stored and then decoded into the HTML data attribute.
